title: ConcatTreesHelper::Empty
description: Helper has accumulated no trees; building yields an empty TokenStream and appending is a no-op.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatTreesHelper

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- self.trees.is_empty() == true
- build(self) constructs TokenStream(None)
- append_to(self, _) returns early without mutating the provided TokenStream

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> NonEmpty via push(tree)
- Empty -> (consumed) via build(self)
- Empty -> (consumed) via append_to(self, &mut TokenStream)

**Evidence:** line 7: trees: Vec<...> stores accumulated trees; emptiness encodes state; line 26: if self.trees.is_empty() branches on the Empty state; line 27: TokenStream(None) returned when Empty; line 34: if self.trees.is_empty() { return; } — append_to is a no-op when Empty

**Implementation:** Encode ConcatTreesHelper<S> with states Empty/NonEmpty via PhantomData. new() -> ConcatTreesHelper<Empty>; push(self, _) -> ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty>; build(self) specialized per state (Empty -> TokenStream<Empty>, NonEmpty -> TokenStream<NonEmpty>); append_to(self, _) only available on NonEmpty so the early return is unnecessary.

title: ConcatTreesHelper::NonEmpty
description: Helper has at least one tree; building/appending performs concatenation and produces/updates a non-empty TokenStream.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatTreesHelper

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- self.trees.is_empty() == false
- build(self) constructs TokenStream(Some(...))
- append_to(self, &mut TokenStream) sets stream.0 = Some(...), making the stream non-empty

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via push(tree)
- NonEmpty -> (consumed) via build(self)
- NonEmpty -> (consumed) via append_to(self, &mut TokenStream)

**Evidence:** line 29: TokenStream(Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees(None, self.trees))) in the non-empty branch of build(); line 37: stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees(stream.0.take(), self.trees)) in append_to() when not empty

**Implementation:** Provide ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty> methods build(self) and append_to(self, &mut TokenStream) only for the NonEmpty state; remove is_empty checks. push() on Empty transitions to NonEmpty at the type level.

title: TokenStream::Empty
description: Represents a token stream with no tokens; encoded as an internal None.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- inner tuple field is None
- appending a NonEmpty ConcatTreesHelper will transition to NonEmpty

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::append_to(self=NonEmpty)
- Constructed as Empty via ConcatTreesHelper::build(self=Empty)

**Evidence:** line 27: TokenStream(None) constructed when helper is empty; line 37: stream.0 = Some(...) assigns Some, demonstrating a transition from possibly None to Some during append_to

**Implementation:** Model TokenStream<S> with states Empty/NonEmpty so operations that require content take TokenStream<NonEmpty>. Provide conversions like ConcatTreesHelper<Empty>::build(self) -> TokenStream<Empty> and ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty>::append_to(self, TokenStream<Empty|NonEmpty>) -> TokenStream<NonEmpty>.

title: TokenStream::NonEmpty
description: Represents a token stream with at least one token; encoded as an internal Some(...)

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- inner tuple field is Some(...)
- appending additional trees keeps the stream in NonEmpty

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::append_to(self=NonEmpty)
- Constructed as NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::build(self=NonEmpty)

**Evidence:** line 29: TokenStream(Some(...)) constructed when helper is non-empty; line 37: stream.0 = Some(...) assigns Some during append_to, preserving NonEmpty

**Implementation:** Use TokenStream<NonEmpty> for APIs that assume content. By distinguishing Empty vs NonEmpty at the type level, callers can’t accidentally rely on empty streams where non-empty is required, and internal Option checks become unnecessary.

title: __rust_start_panic::Aborting (non-returning) behavior
description: In the panic=abort runtime, starting a panic must immediately abort the process and never return or unwind.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_start_panic

**State:** Aborting

**State invariants:**
- Function never returns to its caller
- No stack unwinding or cleanup occurs
- Only optional platform-specific message setting may run before abort

**Evidence:** line 31: comment indicates this shims to the platform abort path; line 33: fn __rust_start_panic(..) -> u32 suggests a return even though it shouldn't; line 47: __rust_abort is declared to return never (!), enforcing divergence once called; line 50: __rust_abort() is invoked unconditionally

**Implementation:** Expose an internal helper with signature fn start_panic_diverging(&mut dyn PanicPayload) -> ! and have the exported symbol delegate to it. This encodes non-returning behavior at the type level, while preserving the required external ABI that returns u32.

title: __rust_panic_cleanup::Unreachable stub
description: In the panic=abort runtime, cleanup for unwinding must never be invoked because unwinding is not linked; this symbol exists only to satisfy linkage expectations.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup

**State:** Unreachable

**State invariants:**
- Function must never be called in panic=abort
- If called, execution hits unreachable!()

**Evidence:** line 27: extern symbol __rust_panic_cleanup is provided for ABI compatibility; line 28: unreachable!() asserts the function is never reached

**Implementation:** If ABI allowed, give the stub a diverging return type (!) to encode that no valid return exists. Otherwise, keep an internal diverging shim and make the exported symbol uncallable within the crate.

title: emscripten catch typeinfo::Unused sentinel
description: A placeholder typeinfo symbol that should never be used under panic=abort; present only to satisfy lang-item/linker expectations on Emscripten.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** personalities::rust_eh_catch_typeinfo

**State:** Unused

**State invariants:**
- Panics do not generate exceptions with panic=abort
- Foreign exceptions are UB with -C panic=abort; catch_unwind will not use this typeinfo

**Evidence:** line 84-89: comments state this typeinfo is only for Emscripten and is never used with panic=abort; line 93: static rust_eh_catch_typeinfo is a zeroed placeholder

**Implementation:** Gate any paths that could reference this symbol behind an explicit Unwinding capability/token that is never constructible in panic=abort builds; this makes use impossible at compile time.

title: TokenStream::Detached (no bridge handle)
description: TokenStream holds no underlying bridge handle (inner Option is None). Acts as an empty stream and cannot perform operations that require a bridge (e.g., expand_expr).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Detached

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == None
- is_empty() returns true unconditionally
- expand_expr() always returns Err(ExpandError)
- into_iter() yields an empty iterator

**Transitions:**
- Detached -> Attached via FromStr::from_str(..) producing TokenStream(Some(..))
- Detached -> Attached via From<TokenTree>::from(..) producing TokenStream(Some(..))

**Evidence:** line 6: pub struct TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>); — Option encodes presence/absence of a handle; line 22-24: new() returns TokenStream(None) — constructs the Detached state; line 29: is_empty() uses unwrap_or(true) — None is treated as empty; line 44: expand_expr() uses self.0.as_ref().ok_or(ExpandError)? — None triggers an immediate error; line 133-136: into_iter() uses unwrap_or_default() when self.0 is None — yields an empty iterator; line 60-62: FromStr::from_str returns TokenStream(Some(..)) — constructs Attached; line 71-73: From<TokenTree> returns TokenStream(Some(..)) — constructs Attached

**Implementation:** Represent as TokenStream<Detached> and TokenStream<Attached>. new() -> TokenStream<Detached>. Conversions like FromStr/From<TokenTree> return TokenStream<Attached>. Methods that require a handle (e.g., expand_expr) are only implemented for TokenStream<Attached>, eliminating the runtime ok_or check.

title: TokenStream::Attached (with bridge handle)
description: TokenStream holds a valid bridge handle (inner Option is Some). Operations that require talking to the bridge are permitted.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Attached

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == Some(handle)
- is_empty() delegates to BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty(handle)
- expand_expr() attempts BridgeMethods::ts_expand_expr and returns TokenStream(Some(..)) on success

**Transitions:**
- Attached -> Attached via expand_expr() Ok(TokenStream(Some(..)))
- Attached -> error via expand_expr() Err(ExpandError)

**Evidence:** line 6: pub struct TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>); — Some(handle) encodes Attached; line 29: is_empty() maps Some(h) to BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty(h); line 44-47: expand_expr() requires Some(..) and on Ok wraps the result as TokenStream(Some(stream)); line 60-62: FromStr::from_str returns TokenStream(Some(..)) — constructs the Attached state; line 71-73: From<TokenTree> returns TokenStream(Some(..)) — constructs the Attached state

**Implementation:** Split into TokenStream<Attached> where inner is the concrete bridge handle (no Option) and TokenStream<Detached> with no handle. Provide expand_expr(&self) -> Result<TokenStream<Attached>, ExpandError> only on Attached. Construction APIs (FromStr/From<TokenTree>) yield Attached directly.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Empty accumulator
description: Helper has collected no inner streams yet; appending is a no-op and building yields an empty TokenStream.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- streams.is_empty() == true
- No empty TokenStream is ever inserted (only Some(inner) are collected)

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Empty via push(empty TokenStream)
- Empty -> Singleton via push(non-empty TokenStream)
- Empty -> (consumed) via append_to(...) (no effect on target stream)
- Empty -> (consumed) via build() producing TokenStream(None)

**Evidence:** line 30: append_to early-returns when self.streams.is_empty(); line 23: build in len <= 1 path returns TokenStream(self.streams.pop()) — when empty, this is TokenStream(None); line 16: push() only inserts when stream.0 is Some(..), skipping empty TokenStream inputs

**Implementation:** Model helper as ConcatStreamsHelper<S> with states Empty/Singleton/Multiple. push(self, NonEmptyTokenStream) transitions Empty->Singleton; append_to/build only available on appropriate states; Empty::append_to/build can be specialized without runtime len checks.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Singleton accumulator
description: Helper holds exactly one non-empty inner stream; building or appending can move it without concatenation work when the base is empty.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Singleton

**State invariants:**
- streams.len() == 1
- Collected inner stream is non-empty (Vec holds bridge::client::TokenStream, not Option)

**Transitions:**
- Singleton -> Multiple via push(non-empty TokenStream)
- Singleton -> (consumed) via build() producing the single inner stream directly
- Singleton -> (consumed) via append_to(base empty) by moving the single stream into target without concatenation
- Singleton -> (consumed) via append_to(base non-empty) by concatenating base with the single stream

**Evidence:** line 22-24: build fast-path when self.streams.len() <= 1 returns TokenStream(self.streams.pop()); line 34-36: append_to fast-path when base.is_none() && self.streams.len() == 1 moves the single inner stream; line 16-17: push() only collects Some(inner), ensuring the stored item is non-empty

**Implementation:** Encode Singleton at the type level (ConcatStreamsHelper<Singleton>) and provide specialized build/append_to that move without calling ts_concat_streams; push transitions to ConcatStreamsHelper<Multiple>.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Multiple accumulator
description: Helper holds more than one non-empty inner stream; building or appending requires concatenation via the bridge.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Multiple

**State invariants:**
- streams.len() > 1
- Concatenation via BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams is required to produce a single stream

**Transitions:**
- Multiple -> (consumed) via build() by calling ts_concat_streams(None, self.streams)
- Multiple -> (consumed) via append_to(base) by calling ts_concat_streams(base, self.streams)

**Evidence:** line 25: build calls BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(None, self.streams) when len > 1; line 37: append_to calls BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(base, self.streams) in the non-fast-path branch

**Implementation:** Specialize ConcatStreamsHelper<Multiple> to always take the concatenation path without runtime len checks; earlier transitions (Empty/Singleton) ensure this state is only constructed with len > 1.

title: TokenStream::Empty state
description: TokenStream that wraps no inner bridge stream (None). Certain operations can fast-path by moving a single collected stream without concatenation.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- inner Option is None
- Appending a single non-empty helper stream can be done by move rather than concatenation

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> NonEmpty via append_to with helper.len() == 1 (move assignment)
- Empty -> NonEmpty via append_to with helper.len() > 1 (concatenation)
- Empty -> Empty via append_to with helper.len() == 0 (no-op)

**Evidence:** line 33-36: base = stream.0.take(); if base.is_none() && self.streams.len() == 1, assign stream.0 = self.streams.pop() (move fast-path); line 30-31: append_to returns early when helper is empty, leaving target (possibly Empty) unchanged; line 23: build returns TokenStream(None) when helper is empty

**Implementation:** Introduce a NonEmptyTokenStream newtype (wrapping the bridge::client::TokenStream) and represent empty as a separate TokenStreamEmpty. Methods like append_to can be overloaded to avoid Option checks and enable move fast-paths at the type level.

title: TokenStream::NonEmpty state
description: TokenStream with an inner bridge stream (Some). Appending additional streams requires concatenation via the bridge.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- inner Option is Some(bridge::client::TokenStream)
- Appending any helper streams uses ts_concat_streams to merge

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via append_to with any non-empty helper (concatenation)

**Evidence:** line 33: base = stream.0.take() captures Some(..) when non-empty; line 37: stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(base, self.streams)) used when base was Some(..) or helper.len() != 1

**Implementation:** Split TokenStream into Empty vs NonEmpty newtypes; define append_to on NonEmpty to always concatenate and on Empty to move when helper is Singleton, eliminating runtime base.is_none() branching.

title: PanicPayload::Unconsumed state
description: The panic payload is still owned by the PanicPayload implementor; it can be taken exactly once and turned into a Box for transfer to the platform unwinder.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_start_panic (PanicPayload usage)

**State:** Unconsumed

**State invariants:**
- take_box() is called exactly once to yield the raw Box allocation pointer
- The raw pointer returned by take_box() must be reconstructed with Box::from_raw before use
- After reconstruction, ownership is moved to the unwinder implementation

**Transitions:**
- Unconsumed -> InFlight via __rust_start_panic() calling Box::from_raw(payload.take_box()) and imp::panic(..)

**Evidence:** line 104: __rust_start_panic(payload: &mut dyn PanicPayload) — receives a mutable handle to the payload to be consumed; line 106: Box::from_raw(payload.take_box()) — exactly-once extraction of the raw allocation and reconstruction into a Box; line 108: imp::panic(payload) — transfers ownership of the Box to the platform-specific unwinder

**Implementation:** Introduce a wrapper StartPanic<'a, S> around &mut dyn PanicPayload with states Unconsumed/Consumed. Implement take(self) -> (Box<dyn Any + Send>, StartPanic<'a, Consumed>) so double-take is a type error; only allow forwarding the Box to imp::panic in the Consumed transition path.

title: PanicPayload::InFlight state
description: After take_box and Box::from_raw, the payload has been moved into the unwinder; the original PanicPayload must not be used again.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_start_panic (PanicPayload usage)

**State:** InFlight

**State invariants:**
- Ownership of the panic object is no longer with the original PanicPayload implementor
- Further use of the &mut dyn PanicPayload after take_box would violate the protocol

**Evidence:** line 106: Box::from_raw(payload.take_box()) — consumes the underlying allocation; line 108: imp::panic(payload) — moves the Box into the platform unwinder (no Box retained locally)

**Implementation:** Model the consumed state as a distinct type (e.g., StartPanic<'a, Consumed>) that does not expose any further operations on the underlying &mut dyn PanicPayload, preventing accidental reuse after transfer.

title: Panic payload pointer::ForeignRaw state
description: A raw pointer to a panic payload provided by the unwinder; valid only if it was produced by the runtime and is passed to cleanup exactly once.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup (FFI payload pointer protocol)

**State:** ForeignRaw

**State invariants:**
- payload must point to a valid panic object constructed by the runtime
- Pointer ownership/provenance must match what imp::cleanup expects
- Caller upholds safety preconditions (function is unsafe and cannot validate this)

**Transitions:**
- ForeignRaw -> BoxedAnyRaw via __rust_panic_cleanup() calling imp::cleanup(payload) and Box::into_raw(..)

**Evidence:** line 97: pub unsafe extern "C" fn __rust_panic_cleanup(payload: *mut u8) — unsafe raw pointer input with no checks; line 98: imp::cleanup(payload) — hands the raw pointer to platform-specific code to reconstruct a Box

**Implementation:** Wrap the raw pointer in a ForeignPanicPtr newtype that can only be constructed at the FFI boundary and exposes a single cleanup(self) -> Box<dyn Any + Send> method, encoding the one-time use at the type level.

title: Panic payload pointer::BoxedAnyRaw state
description: An owning raw pointer to Box<dyn Any + Send> returned to the caller; it must be converted back with Box::from_raw exactly once and then properly dropped.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup (FFI payload pointer protocol)

**State:** BoxedAnyRaw

**State invariants:**
- Pointer uniquely owns the heap allocation backing dyn Any + Send
- Caller is responsible for exactly-once Box::from_raw and eventual drop
- Leaking or double-freeing is possible if the protocol is violated

**Evidence:** line 98: Box::into_raw(imp::cleanup(payload)) — converts a Box into an owning raw pointer returned to the caller

**Implementation:** Return a RAII wrapper (e.g., OwnedPanicBox) instead of *mut Trait that implements Drop by reconstructing Box::from_raw and dropping, or require callers to accept and return a typed newtype that ensures single-drop semantics.

title: test_rng::ValidSeed construction
description: The RNG is created only if a 16-byte seed is successfully constructed from the caller location hash and a fixed 8-byte suffix.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** test_helpers::test_rng

**State:** ValidSeed

**State invariants:**
- seed_vec length is exactly 16 bytes
- try_into::<[u8;16]>() does not fail
- XorShiftRng::from_seed receives a [u8;16] seed

**Evidence:** line 105: seed_vec built by chaining 8 bytes from hc64.to_le_bytes() with 8 bytes from 0u8..8; line 106: seed_vec.as_slice().try_into().unwrap() — unwrap will panic if length != 16; line 107: rand::SeedableRng::from_seed(seed) requires exactly [u8; 16]

**Implementation:** Avoid the runtime try_into().unwrap() by constructing the [u8; 16] directly: let le = hc64.to_le_bytes(); let seed = [le[0], le[1], le[2], le[3], le[4], le[5], le[6], le[7], 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]; rand::SeedableRng::from_seed(seed). Alternatively, introduce a Seed16 newtype that can only be constructed from sources guaranteeing 16 bytes.

title: Punct::Construction precondition (allowed character set)
description: Punct::new only accepts punctuation characters from a fixed allowlist; otherwise it panics. This guarantees the stored byte fits the ASCII punctuation range and the internal u8 cast is sound.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** AllowedChar

**State invariants:**
- input ch is in LEGAL_CHARS
- ch as u8 is lossless (ASCII punctuation only)
- constructed Punct always represents a language-permitted punctuation

**Transitions:**
- AllowedChar -> Punct constructed via Punct::new(ch, spacing)
- DisallowedChar -> panic! in Punct::new

**Evidence:** line 28: LEGAL_CHARS contains the allowlist for punctuation; line 32: if !LEGAL_CHARS.contains(&ch) { ... } runtime guard; line 33: panic!("unsupported character `{:?}`", ch) names the precondition; line 36: ch is downcast to u8, relying on the allowlist for correctness

**Implementation:** Introduce an AllowedPunctChar newtype (TryFrom<char>) that validates membership in LEGAL_CHARS. Change Punct::new to accept AllowedPunctChar or implement TryFrom<char> for Punct (returning Result) to eliminate the panic and make the precondition explicit in the type.

title: Punct::Joint spacing state
description: A Punct constructed with Spacing::Joint is in the Joint spacing state, meaning it may combine with a following token to form a multi-character operator.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** Joint

**State invariants:**
- self.0.joint == true
- spacing() returns Spacing::Joint

**Transitions:**
- Unspecified -> Joint via Punct::new(ch, Spacing::Joint)

**Evidence:** line 37: joint: spacing == Spacing::Joint encodes the state as a bool; line 52: spacing() branches on self.0.joint; line 48: doc explains Joint may combine into a multi-character operator

**Implementation:** Model as Punct<S> with zero-sized states Joint/Alone (PhantomData). Provide constructors new_joint/new_alone. spacing() becomes a type-level fact, avoiding runtime branching and making state explicit to downstream APIs.

title: Punct::Alone spacing state
description: A Punct constructed with Spacing::Alone is in the Alone spacing state, meaning the operator has definitively ended and will not combine with the next token.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** Alone

**State invariants:**
- self.0.joint == false
- spacing() returns Spacing::Alone

**Transitions:**
- Unspecified -> Alone via Punct::new(ch, Spacing::Alone)

**Evidence:** line 37: joint: spacing == Spacing::Joint implies Alone when false; line 52-54: spacing() returns Spacing::Alone when self.0.joint is false; line 49-50: doc explains Alone means the operator has ended

**Implementation:** Use Punct<Alone> and Punct<Joint> phantom-state types. Methods that depend on spacing (e.g., builder/concat helpers) can be constrained to specific states, removing the need to inspect a runtime bool.

title: Punct::Constructed (valid punctuation char)
description: Punct::new requires that ch is one of the language-permitted punctuation characters; otherwise it panics. After construction, the character is guaranteed to be in the allowed set.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** Constructed

**State invariants:**
- ch ∈ LEGAL_CHARS (language-permitted punctuation)
- initial span is Span::call_site()
- spacing flag is encoded as joint == (spacing == Spacing::Joint)

**Evidence:** line 159: doc states 'The ch argument must be a valid punctuation character permitted by the language, otherwise the function will panic.'; line 166: const LEGAL_CHARS lists the only allowed characters; line 170: runtime check `if !LEGAL_CHARS.contains(&ch)` enforces the precondition; line 171: panic! with message "unsupported character ..." reveals the invariant; line 176: constructor sets span to Span::call_site().0

**Implementation:** Introduce a ValidPunctChar newtype (constructed via TryFrom<char>) that validates membership in LEGAL_CHARS. Change Punct::new to accept ValidPunctChar (or provide Punct::try_new returning Result), eliminating the runtime panic and making invalid construction a type/return-typed error.

title: Ident::Valid identifier (non-raw)
description: Ident::new requires that the provided string is a language-valid identifier; otherwise it panics. After construction, the identifier is guaranteed valid and NFC-normalized.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Valid

**State invariants:**
- string is a valid Rust identifier (keywords allowed)
- identifier is NFC-normalized
- is_raw == false for this constructor

**Evidence:** line 249: doc states the string must be a valid identifier permitted by the language; line 250: doc states 'Otherwise, the function will panic.'; line 252: doc mentions the constructed identifier will be NFC-normalized; line 274: constructor sets is_raw: false

**Implementation:** Define a ValidIdentStr newtype (TryFrom<&str>) that performs lexical validation/normalization. Provide Ident::from(ValidIdentStr, Span) or impl TryFrom<(&str, Span)> for Ident to return Result instead of panicking, preventing invalid construction by type/return type.

title: Ident::Raw identifier (restricted keywords)
description: Ident::new_raw requires a valid identifier string for raw identifiers; some keywords usable in path segments (e.g., self, super) are explicitly disallowed and will cause a panic.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Raw

**State invariants:**
- string is a valid raw identifier (not one of the disallowed path-segment keywords)
- is_raw == true for this constructor

**Evidence:** line 279: doc clarifies this is 'Same as Ident::new, but creates a raw identifier'; line 281: doc notes certain path-segment keywords are not supported; line 282: doc states that using unsupported keywords 'will cause a panic'; line 286: constructor sets sym via new_ident(..., true); line 287: constructor sets is_raw: true

**Implementation:** Introduce a RawIdentStr newtype (TryFrom<&str>) that rejects path-segment keywords and only allows valid raw identifiers. Change Ident::new_raw to accept RawIdentStr (or provide try_new_raw -> Result<Ident, Error>) to avoid panics and make invalid inputs unrepresentable or handled via Result.

title: Ident::NonRaw identifier
description: Identifier constructed without the raw (r#) prefix semantics.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** NonRaw

**State invariants:**
- is_raw == false in the underlying bridge::Ident
- string argument was a valid identifier per language rules; otherwise construction would have panicked
- constructed identifier is NFC-normalized
- a Span was provided at construction and can later be changed via set_span()

**Evidence:** line 33: pub fn new(string: &str, span: Span) -> Ident; line 35: Symbol::new_ident(string, false) — rawness flag set to false; line 36: is_raw: false — runtime boolean encodes the NonRaw state; line 11: "The `string` argument must be a valid identifier... Otherwise, the function will panic." — runtime precondition; line 14: "The constructed identifier will be NFC-normalized."

**Implementation:** Introduce Ident<S> with zero-sized markers Raw and NonRaw. Make new() -> Ident<NonRaw> and new_raw() -> Ident<Raw>. Only Ident<Raw> would print with r# and carry raw semantics; the boolean is_raw can be eliminated. Optionally, accept a ValidIdentStr newtype or TryFrom<&str> to avoid panics during construction.

title: Ident::Raw identifier
description: Identifier constructed with raw (r#ident) semantics to allow otherwise reserved words.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Raw

**State invariants:**
- is_raw == true in the underlying bridge::Ident
- string argument must be a valid identifier; certain path-segment keywords (e.g., self, super) are disallowed and will panic
- constructed identifier is NFC-normalized
- a Span was provided at construction and can later be changed via set_span()

**Evidence:** line 41: Docstring states this creates a raw identifier (r#ident); line 42: "The `string` argument be a valid identifier..."; line 43: "Keywords which are usable in path segments (e.g. `self`, `super`) are not supported, and will cause a panic." — runtime precondition; line 48: Symbol::new_ident(string, true) — rawness flag set to true; line 49: is_raw: true — runtime boolean encodes the Raw state; line 14: "The constructed identifier will be NFC-normalized."

**Implementation:** Split into Ident<Raw> and Ident<NonRaw>; have new_raw() -> Ident<Raw> and new() -> Ident<NonRaw>. Expose APIs that differ for rawness only on the corresponding state. Replace panic-on-invalid with a TryFrom<&str> for a ValidIdentStr newtype to avoid runtime panics during construction.

title: Span::DefSite hygiene
description: A Span created to resolve at the macro definition site (definition-site hygiene).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** DefSite

**State invariants:**
- Identifiers created with this Span resolve as if at the macro definition site
- Represents a distinct hygiene context from call_site() and mixed_site()

**Transitions:**
- DefSite -> location of `other` via located_at(other)
- DefSite -> resolution of `other` via resolved_at(other)

**Evidence:** line 20: "A span that resolves at the macro definition site."; line 22-24: def_site() constructor establishes this hygiene state

**Implementation:** Introduce Span<H> with zero-sized hygiene markers H = DefSite, CallSite, MixedSite. def_site() returns Span<DefSite>. Methods like resolved_at/located_at change H accordingly, so code can't accidentally mix hygiene contexts without an explicit transition.

title: Span::CallSite hygiene
description: A Span representing the macro invocation site (call-site hygiene).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** CallSite

**State invariants:**
- Identifiers created with this span resolve as if written at the macro call location
- Code at the macro call site can refer to such identifiers (per docs)

**Transitions:**
- CallSite -> location of `other` via located_at(other)
- CallSite -> resolution of `other` via resolved_at(other)

**Evidence:** line 26-33: docs describe call-site hygiene semantics for call_site(); line 31-33: call_site() constructor establishes this hygiene state

**Implementation:** Use Span<CallSite> as a distinct type parameter. Only explicit methods can switch to another hygiene context, preventing accidental mixing at compile time.

title: Span::MixedSite hygiene
description: A Span representing macro_rules hygiene that sometimes resolves at def-site and sometimes at call-site; location comes from the call-site.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** MixedSite

**State invariants:**
- May resolve at macro definition site for locals/labels/$crate
- May resolve at call site for other names
- Location is taken from call site

**Transitions:**
- MixedSite -> location of `other` via located_at(other)
- MixedSite -> resolution of `other` via resolved_at(other)

**Evidence:** line 35-42: docs describe mixed-site hygiene behavior and location source; line 40-42: mixed_site() constructor establishes this hygiene state

**Implementation:** As above, a Span<MixedSite> marker makes this hygiene context explicit in the type. Conversions to other contexts go through explicit methods.

title: Span::Generated-by-expansion
description: Span values that were produced by a previous macro expansion (they have a parent).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** HasParentExpansion

**State invariants:**
- parent() returns Some(previous expansion span)
- source() returns the origin source span (which may differ from self)

**Evidence:** line 44-49: parent(): "previous macro expansion ... if any" and returns Option<Span>; line 51-57: source(): returns origin source span; differs if generated from expansions

**Implementation:** Refine Span into Span<Generated> and Span<Original>. parent(&Span<Generated>) -> Span; source(&Span<Generated>) -> Span<Original>. Constructing Span<Generated> only via APIs that attest it was expansion-produced.

title: Span::Original-source
description: Span values that were not generated from other macro expansions (no parent).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** NoParentExpansion

**State invariants:**
- parent() returns None
- source() returns the same Span as self

**Evidence:** line 44-49: parent() returns None when there is no previous expansion; line 51-56: source(): "if this Span wasn't generated ... return value is the same as *self"

**Implementation:** Use a Span<Original> marker to denote no-expansion provenance so APIs depending on original-source provenance can require it at compile time.

title: Span::Joinable with other (same file)
description: join(self, other) only succeeds when both spans originate from the same file.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** SameFileWith(other)

**State invariants:**
- join(self, other) returns Some if and only if both are from the same file
- If this holds, the resulting span encompasses self and other

**Evidence:** line 112-115: docs: "Returns None if `self` and `other` are from different files."; line 116-118: join(&self, other: Span) -> Option<Span> encodes this at runtime

**Implementation:** Introduce a SameFile witness token obtained from a checked operation (e.g., try_same_file(self, other) -> Option<SameFile>). Define join(self, other, _w: SameFile) -> Span so the call is only possible when a witness proves same-file at compile time.

title: Span::Not joinable with other (different files)
description: join(self, other) must not produce a span when spans are from different files.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** DifferentFileFrom(other)

**State invariants:**
- join(self, other) returns None
- No encompassing span exists across different files

**Evidence:** line 112-115: explicit comment: "Returns None if `self` and `other` are from different files."

**Implementation:** Model file identity in the type (e.g., Span<F> where F is a file marker). Provide join only for Span<F> with the same F, making cross-file joins a compile error instead of a runtime None.

title: Span::Backed by local filesystem path
description: Only spans that correspond to an actual on-disk file have a local filesystem path.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** HasLocalFilePath

**State invariants:**
- local_file() returns Some(PathBuf)
- Path is unaffected by remapping and intended for local use (not embedding)

**Evidence:** line 102-107: docs describe local_file() as the actual path on disk and caution its use; line 108-110: local_file(&self) -> Option<PathBuf> indicates conditional availability

**Implementation:** Provide a LocalFileSpan newtype constructed via TryFrom<Span>. Only LocalFileSpan exposes local_path(&self) -> &Path, so call sites that require a real path are type-checked.

title: Span::Not backed by local filesystem path
description: Spans from remapped or artificial paths do not have a local filesystem path.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** NoLocalFilePath

**State invariants:**
- local_file() returns None
- Only file() (display path) is available

**Evidence:** line 93-100: file() may be remapped or artificial; line 102-110: local_file() returns a path on disk; absence implies remapped/artificial origin

**Implementation:** Separate Span into Span<DisplayOnly> and LocalFileSpan; APIs that need a real path require LocalFileSpan, preventing accidental reliance on remapped paths.

title: Span::Real source text available
description: Source text is only available for spans that correspond to real source code.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** HasRealSourceText

**State invariants:**
- source_text() returns Some(String)
- Returned text preserves original spaces and comments
- Intended for diagnostics only

**Evidence:** line 140-147: docs: returns source text; only returns a result if the span corresponds to real source code; line 148-150: source_text(&self) -> Option<String> encodes conditional availability

**Implementation:** Introduce a RealSourceSpan newtype via TryFrom<Span>. Only RealSourceSpan exposes source_text_nonempty(&self) -> &str, avoiding repeated Option handling and accidental reliance on non-real sources.

title: Span::No real source text
description: Spans that do not correspond to real source code have no retrievable source text.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** NoSourceText

**State invariants:**
- source_text() returns None
- Diagnostics must not rely on unavailable text

**Evidence:** line 141-147: explicitly conditional availability of source text

**Implementation:** APIs that require text could accept RealSourceSpan only; callers must explicitly check and convert from Span first.

title: Span::Saved/recovered ID protocol
description: recover_proc_macro_span(id) expects an id that was previously returned by save_span(); passing arbitrary ids is undefined/misleading.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** SavedIdValid

**State invariants:**
- Only ids produced by save_span(&self) are valid for recover_proc_macro_span(id)
- The id is an opaque handle for the current proc-macro context

**Evidence:** line 152-156: save_span(&self) -> usize (doc-hidden) used by Span::quote; line 159-164: recover_proc_macro_span(id: usize) -> Span (doc-hidden) pairs with save_span; line 152 and 159: comments indicate intended pairing and internal-only protocol

**Implementation:** Replace usize with a newtype SavedSpanId that is !Send + !Sync and only constructible by save_span(); recover_proc_macro_span takes SavedSpanId. Optionally tie SavedSpanId to a session lifetime via PhantomData to prevent cross-session misuse.

title: Delimiter::None usage constraints
description: Delimiter::None is an invisible delimiter with special, fragile behavior in proc_macro output; grouping may be ignored by rustc, so it should generally not be used when constructing output that relies on preserved operator precedence.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Delimiter

**State:** None

**State invariants:**
- In proc_macro output, None-delimited groups may have their grouping ignored by rustc
- Only None-delimited groups originating from macro_rules input are sometimes preserved, and only in very specific circumstances
- To preserve operator priorities in output, prefer Parenthesis/Brace/Bracket over None

**Evidence:** line 20: "Invisible delimiters might not survive roundtrip of a token stream through a string."; line 24: "rustc currently can ignore the grouping of tokens delimited by `None` in the output of a proc_macro."; lines 25-26: "Only `None`-delimited groups created by a macro_rules macro in the input of a proc_macro macro are preserved, and only in very specific circumstances."; lines 27-29: "Any `None`-delimited groups (re)created by a proc_macro will therefore not preserve operator priorities... The other `Delimiter` variants should be used instead in this context."

**Implementation:** Introduce a NonNoneDelimiter newtype or an OutputDelimiter enum that excludes the None variant and use it in APIs that construct proc_macro output. Require explicit, opt-in conversion to use Delimiter::None (e.g., via an unsafe or clearly named escape hatch), so typical output construction cannot choose None at compile time.

title: Spacing::Joint validity in parsed token streams
description: In token streams parsed from source, Joint may only be assigned when the punct is immediately followed (no whitespace) by another punct, or when a single quote is immediately followed by an identifier. In constructed token streams (via proc-macro APIs), no such restriction applies.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Spacing

**State:** Joint

**State invariants:**
- Parsed source: Joint only if next token is a Punct with no intervening whitespace, or a single quote immediately followed by an identifier with no whitespace
- Constructed streams: Joint punctuation can be followed by any other tokens
- Joint implies the punct can join with the following token to form a multi-character operator

**Evidence:** line 7: "A `Punct` token can join with the following token to form a multi-character operator."; line 9: "In token streams constructed using proc macro interfaces, `Joint` punctuation tokens can be followed by any other tokens."; line 10-11: "in token streams parsed from source code, the compiler will only set spacing to `Joint` in the following cases."; line 12-13: Case 1 — Punct immediately followed by another Punct without whitespace (e.g., '+=' and '++'); line 14-15: Case 2 — Single quote immediately followed by an identifier without whitespace (e.g., 'lifetime)

**Implementation:** Differentiate parsed vs constructed origins at the type level (e.g., Punct<Parsed> vs Punct<Constructed> or Spacing<Origin>). Only the parsed constructor computes/permits Spacing::Joint under the documented adjacency rules; the constructed variant allows arbitrary spacing. This removes the need to rely on comments/conventions about origin when assigning Spacing.

title: Spacing::Alone default and end-of-stream invariant in parsed token streams
description: In parsed token streams, Alone is assigned in all cases not covered by the Joint rules; in particular, a punct not followed by anything must be Alone. Alone punctuation can be followed by any tokens.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Spacing

**State:** Alone

**State invariants:**
- Parsed source: spacing is Alone in all cases not covered by the Joint preconditions
- End-of-stream: tokens not followed by anything are marked as Alone
- Alone punctuation can be followed by any tokens

**Evidence:** line 20: "A `Punct` token cannot join with the following token to form a multi-character operator."; line 22-24: "the compiler will set spacing to `Alone` in all cases not covered by the conditions for `Joint` above" with examples; line 25: "In particular, tokens not followed by anything will be marked as `Alone`."

**Implementation:** Introduce an origin/state parameter (e.g., Parsed vs Constructed) for tokens so that in the Parsed state constructors enforce that the final token in a sequence is created with Spacing::Alone and that Alone is the default when Joint preconditions are not met. This encodes the origin-dependent invariant in types rather than comments.

title: Literal::Char kind state
description: A Literal representing a character; only character-specific operations are valid.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** Char

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::Char
- character_value() succeeds for this kind
- Stringification uses the `'c'` form

**Evidence:** line 165: Literal::character constructs with bridge::LitKind::Char; line 301-307: character_value() matches on bridge::LitKind::Char and returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind) otherwise; line 265-266: with_stringify_parts uses the Char branch to format `'…'`

**Implementation:** Define Literal<K> with marker types (e.g., K=Char). character_value() only exists for Literal<Char>. Provide fallible TryFrom<Literal> -> Literal<Char> when starting from an untyped Literal.

title: Literal::Byte (byte character) kind state
description: A Literal representing a byte character; only byte-character-specific operations are valid.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** Byte

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::Byte
- Stringification uses the b'c' form
- Calling value accessors for other kinds should not be permitted

**Evidence:** line 170-178: byte_character() constructs with bridge::LitKind::Byte; line 264-265: with_stringify_parts handles the Byte branch as b'…'; line 288-296: byte_character_value() returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind) when kind mismatches (doc indicates it is for byte character literals)

**Implementation:** Introduce Literal<ByteChar>. Provide byte_character_value() only for Literal<ByteChar>. Converting from an untyped Literal requires checking the kind once.

title: Literal::Str (cooked string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a cooked string; str_value() unescapes as needed.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** Str

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::Str
- str_value() returns Ok(String), potentially unescaping
- Stringification uses the "…" form

**Evidence:** line 146-154: string() constructs with bridge::LitKind::Str; line 313-341: str_value() matches Str and StrRaw, returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind) otherwise; line 266-267: with_stringify_parts formats cooked strings as "…"

**Implementation:** Use Literal<StrCooked> and Literal<StrRaw<N>> marker types. Implement str_value() only for the appropriate typed variants.

title: Literal::StrRaw (raw string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a raw string; str_value() returns the raw content directly.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** StrRaw

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::StrRaw(_)
- str_value() returns Ok(String) without unescaping
- Stringification uses r#"…"# form with appropriate number of #

**Evidence:** line 338: str_value() returns Ok for StrRaw(_); line 267-270: with_stringify_parts handles StrRaw(n) formatting with n hashes

**Implementation:** Represent as Literal<StrRaw<N>> (const-generic) or a StrRaw marker; only expose str_value() on these typed literals.

title: Literal::CStr (cooked C string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a cooked C string; cstr_value() unescapes and appends a NUL terminator.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** CStr

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::CStr
- cstr_value() returns Ok(Vec<u8>) with a trailing 0 byte
- Stringification uses c"…" form

**Evidence:** line 194-202: c_string() constructs with bridge::LitKind::CStr; line 347-369: cstr_value() matches CStr and processes escapes; returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind) otherwise; line 276-277: with_stringify_parts formats CStr as c"…"

**Implementation:** Use Literal<CStrCooked> and only implement cstr_value() for that type; provide TryFrom<Literal> for safe downcasting.

title: Literal::CStrRaw (raw C string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a raw C string; cstr_value() appends NUL without unescaping.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** CStrRaw

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::CStrRaw(_)
- cstr_value() returns Ok(Vec<u8>) with trailing 0, without unescaping
- Stringification uses cr#"…"#

**Evidence:** line 370-377: cstr_value() branch handles CStrRaw(_) without escapes and appends NUL; line 277-280: with_stringify_parts formats CStrRaw(n) with n hashes

**Implementation:** Represent as Literal<CStrRaw<N>> (const-generic). Implement cstr_value() only for this typed variant.

title: Literal::ByteStr (cooked byte string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a cooked byte string; byte_str_value() unescapes to bytes.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** ByteStr

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::ByteStr
- byte_str_value() returns Ok(Vec<u8>) for this kind
- Stringification uses b"…"

**Evidence:** line 182-190: byte_string() constructs with bridge::LitKind::ByteStr; line 386-391: byte_str_value() begins by matching on ByteStr; line 271-272: with_stringify_parts formats ByteStr as b"…"

**Implementation:** Use Literal<ByteStrCooked> and only implement byte_str_value() for that type.

title: Literal::ByteStrRaw (raw byte string) kind state
description: A Literal representing a raw byte string; value access should return raw bytes without unescaping.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** ByteStrRaw

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::ByteStrRaw(_)
- Stringification uses br#"…"# form

**Evidence:** line 273-275: with_stringify_parts handles ByteStrRaw(n) formatting; line 382-385: byte_str_value() docs state it handles byte string literals (raw included)

**Implementation:** Represent as Literal<ByteStrRaw<N>>; only implement the appropriate value method on this typed variant.

title: Literal::Float constructors require finite input
description: Float literal constructors panic if provided NaN or infinite values; only finite floats are accepted.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** FloatConstructionPrecondition

**State invariants:**
- Inputs to f32_* and f64_* constructors must satisfy is_finite()
- On violation, constructors panic at runtime

**Evidence:** line 68-70: f32_unsuffixed panics if !n.is_finite(); line 93-95: f32_suffixed panics if !n.is_finite(); line 113-115: f64_unsuffixed panics if !n.is_finite(); line 138-140: f64_suffixed panics if !n.is_finite()

**Implementation:** Introduce FiniteF32/FiniteF64 newtypes (like NotNaN) constructible via TryFrom<f32/f64>; change constructors to accept FiniteF32/FiniteF64, eliminating panics and making non-finite inputs a type error at call sites when using typed wrappers.

title: Literal::subspan UTF-8 boundary and mapping assumptions
description: subspan() should be given ranges that map within the literal's source and start/end at UTF-8 boundaries; otherwise later operations may panic.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** SubspanCallPreconditions

**State invariants:**
- Requested range must be within self.span()
- Ideally, start/end should be on UTF-8 boundaries (not currently enforced)

**Evidence:** line 216-218: docs define bounds requirement for range, returning None if out of bounds; line 219-221: FIXME notes lack of UTF-8 boundary checks, risk of later panic; line 222-226: FIXME notes users cannot know exact mapping, implying a protocol hazard

**Implementation:** Return a BoundaryRange token produced by the compiler that encodes valid UTF-8 boundaries for a Span, or make subspan validate UTF-8 boundaries and return Result<Span, Error> to avoid latent panics.

title: FilteredTests::Consistent ID assignment
description: The struct maintains a global, monotonically increasing TestId across both tests and benches using next_id. All insertions must mint the current next_id as the element's TestId and then increment exactly once.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Consistent

**State invariants:**
- Each (TestId, TestDescAndFn) in tests and benches has a unique TestId
- TestIds are assigned in insertion order across both vectors (global sequencing shared by tests and benches)
- next_id is always the next unused id (equal to the number of ids already issued since initialization)
- total_len() equals tests.len() + benches.len(); next_id should be >= total_len() and typically equals initial_next_id + total_len() if only add_* are used

**Transitions:**
- Consistent -> Consistent via add_bench() (assign TestId(self.next_id) then increment)
- Consistent -> Consistent via add_test() (assign TestId(self.next_id) then increment)

**Evidence:** line 6: struct FilteredTests holds tests, benches, and a next_id counter; line 15: benches push uses TestId(self.next_id) — id minted from the shared counter; line 16: self.next_id += 1 — counter increment after insertion (benches); line 20: tests push uses TestId(self.next_id) — same shared counter for tests; line 21: self.next_id += 1 — counter increment after insertion (tests); line 24: total_len() sums tests and benches, implying coupling between lengths and ids issued

**Implementation:** Encapsulate the counter as a private NextId newtype with fn issue(&mut self) -> TestId that returns the current id and increments. Keep tests/benches private and only allow insertion via methods that call issue(); optionally expose a finalize() that returns an immutable collection to prevent further mutation. This enforces unique, monotonic id issuance at compile time by eliminating ad hoc counter manipulation.

title: FilteredTests::Empty
description: Freshly constructed with no tests or benches and the next_id counter at 0.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- tests.is_empty() and benches.is_empty()
- next_id == 0
- total_len() == 0

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Populated via add_test(desc, testfn)
- Empty -> Populated via add_bench(desc, testfn)

**Evidence:** line: let mut filtered = FilteredTests { tests: Vec::new(), benches: Vec::new(), next_id: 0 };; line: fn total_len(&self) -> usize { self.tests.len() + self.benches.len() }

**Implementation:** Remove next_id and derive TestId from the current total_len() at insertion time (e.g., let id = TestId(self.total_len());). Alternatively, encapsulate ID allocation in a dedicated IdAllocator capability that only hands out unique, sequential TestId values, preventing violation of the invariant by construction.

title: FilteredTests::Populated
description: After any number of add_test/add_bench calls, the internal counter must match the number of stored entries and TestId uniqueness/sequencing is assumed.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Populated

**State invariants:**
- next_id == tests.len() + benches.len()
- Assigned TestId values are unique and sequential in insertion order
- Tests and benches are partitioned into separate vectors

**Transitions:**
- Populated -> Populated via add_test(desc, testfn) (increments next_id, pushes to tests)
- Populated -> Populated via add_bench(desc, testfn) (increments next_id, pushes to benches)

**Evidence:** line: fn add_test(&mut self, desc: TestDesc, testfn: TestFn) { ... self.tests.push((TestId(self.next_id), test)); self.next_id += 1; }; line: fn add_bench(&mut self, desc: TestDesc, testfn: TestFn) { ... self.benches.push((TestId(self.next_id), test)); self.next_id += 1; }; line: fn total_len(&self) -> usize { self.tests.len() + self.benches.len() }

**Implementation:** Replace next_id with deriving the id from the container size, or move id generation into an IdAllocator capability passed to add_* so TestId cannot be constructed incorrectly. This removes the need to maintain the next_id == total_len() invariant at runtime.

title: Static-only precondition for test_main_static
description: The static entrypoint only accepts static tests/benches; passing dynamic tests is a logic error and results in a panic.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** test_main_static/make_owned_test

**State:** StaticOnly

**State invariants:**
- All entries must be StaticTestFn or StaticBenchFn
- Dynamic tests are rejected at runtime with panic

**Evidence:** line: fn make_owned_test(test: &&TestDescAndFn) -> TestDescAndFn { match test.testfn { StaticTestFn(f) => ..., StaticBenchFn(f) => ..., _ => panic!("non-static tests passed to test::test_main_static"), } }; line: pub fn test_main_static(tests: &[&TestDescAndFn]) { ... tests.iter().map(make_owned_test) ... }

**Implementation:** Introduce a StaticTestDescAndFn newtype that can only be constructed from StaticTestFn/StaticBenchFn, and change test_main_static to accept &[&StaticTestDescAndFn]. This makes dynamic tests unrepresentable at the type level, turning the current runtime panic into a compile-time guarantee.

title: Child process static-only and no-bench preconditions
description: When running the child-process path (spawned secondary), only a single static test may be executed; dynamic tests and benchmarks are rejected with panics.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** test_main_static_abort (child-process path)

**State:** ChildProcess

**State invariants:**
- Env var __RUST_TEST_INVOKE selects exactly one test by name
- Runnable::Test must be static; dynamic tests panic
- Runnable::Bench is not allowed in child process and panics

**Evidence:** line: if let Ok(name) = env::var(SECONDARY_TEST_INVOKER_VAR) { ... }; line: match testfn.into_runnable() { Runnable::Test(runnable_test) => { if runnable_test.is_dynamic() { panic!("only static tests are supported"); } ... }; line: Runnable::Bench(_) => { panic!("benchmarks should not be executed into child processes") }; line: .find(|test| test.desc.name.as_slice() == name).unwrap_or_else(|| panic!("couldn't find a test with the provided name '{name}'"))

**Implementation:** Model the child-process execution context with a ChildProcess capability that exposes only a run_static_test(desc, StaticRunnableTest) entrypoint; its API should not accept benches or dynamic tests. Construct this capability only when the env var is present, removing the need for runtime panics.

title: RunningTest::NotJoined
description: Represents a launched test thread that must be joined to observe completion/panics.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** RunningTest (local to run_tests)

**State:** NotJoined

**State invariants:**
- join_handle == Some(handle)
- Caller must call join(self) exactly once to cleanly observe thread completion

**Transitions:**
- NotJoined -> Joined via join(self)

**Evidence:** line: struct RunningTest { join_handle: Option<thread::JoinHandle<()>>, }; line: let join_handle = run_test(...); ... RunningTest { join_handle }; line: fn join(self, completed_test: &mut CompletedTest) { if let Some(join_handle) = self.join_handle { if let Err(_) = join_handle.join() { ... } } }

**Implementation:** Encode RunningTest<S> with states Running and Joined; run_test returns RunningTest<Running>, and join(self) -> RunningTest<Joined>. Only Running exposes join; Joined cannot be joined again and carries no JoinHandle, preventing misuse without Option checks.

title: RunningTest::Joined
description: Terminal state after joining; no further thread interaction is valid.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** RunningTest (local to run_tests)

**State:** Joined

**State invariants:**
- Underlying thread has terminated
- No JoinHandle remains

**Evidence:** line: fn join(self, ...) consumes self and (if Some) calls join_handle.join(); line: join(self) updates completed_test.result if the thread panicked after reporting success

**Implementation:** RunningTest<Joined> is a ZST with no methods to re-join; APIs accept only the appropriate state parameter, preventing double-join or use-after-join at compile time.

title: TokenStream::Empty (no bridge handle)
description: Represents a token stream with no underlying bridge handle; treated as empty and many operations degrade (e.g., expansion fails early).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == None (no underlying bridge handle)
- is_empty() returns true
- Display produces no output (writes nothing)
- expand_expr() always returns Err(ExpandError)

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Backed via From<TokenTree>::from() (creates Some(handle))
- Empty -> Backed via FromStr::from_str() (parsing returns Some(handle))
- Empty -> Backed via FromIterator<TokenTree>::from_iter() when iterator non-empty
- Empty -> Backed via Extend<TokenTree>::extend() when iterator non-empty
- Empty -> Backed via Extend<TokenStream>::extend() when appended streams are non-empty

**Evidence:** line 107: struct TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>) — Option encodes presence/absence of handle; line 163-165: TokenStream::new() returns TokenStream(None); line 169-171: is_empty uses unwrap_or(true) — None treated as empty; line 221-227: Display matches on self.0; None => Ok(()) (prints nothing); line 185: expand_expr uses self.0.as_ref().ok_or(ExpandError)? — None yields Err; line 289-295: ConcatTreesHelper::build returns TokenStream(None) when no trees; line 297-303: ConcatTreesHelper::append_to sets stream.0 = Some(...) only when there are trees; line 204-206: FromStr builds TokenStream(Some(...)); line 262-265: From<TokenTree> returns TokenStream(Some(...)); line 357-363: FromIterator<TokenStream> builds from collected inner streams; empty input yields TokenStream(None); line 316-320: ConcatStreamsHelper::push ignores TokenStream(None) inputs

**Implementation:** Introduce TokenStream<S> with zero-sized states: Empty and Backed. new() -> TokenStream<Empty>. Parsing/From<TokenTree>/collect return TokenStream<Backed>. Implement extend to return/produce TokenStream<Backed> when adding any items. Provide expand_expr only on TokenStream<Backed>, eliminating the need for runtime ok_or checks.

title: TokenStream::Backed (has bridge handle)
description: Represents a token stream backed by a bridge handle; operations delegate to bridge methods. Stream may still be logically empty, but a handle is present.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Backed

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == Some(handle)
- Display prints via BridgeMethods::ts_to_string(handle)
- expand_expr() attempts BridgeMethods::ts_expand_expr(handle) and returns Ok(TokenStream(Some(...))) or Err(ExpandError)
- is_empty() delegates to BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty(handle)

**Transitions:**
- Backed -> Backed via Extend<TokenTree>::extend() with non-empty input
- Backed -> Backed via Extend<TokenStream>::extend() with non-empty input

**Evidence:** line 107: struct TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>) — Some(handle) indicates backed state; line 221-225: Display for TokenStream uses BridgeMethods::ts_to_string(ts) when Some; line 184-189: expand_expr obtains handle (ok_or) and calls BridgeMethods::ts_expand_expr; wraps result in TokenStream(Some(...)) on success; line 169-171: is_empty delegates to BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty when Some; line 330-339: ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to sets stream.0 = Some(...) when appending streams; line 297-303: ConcatTreesHelper::append_to sets stream.0 = Some(...) when appending trees

**Implementation:** Model TokenStream<Backed> as the only state with methods that require a handle (e.g., expand_expr, Display). Conversions and builders return TokenStream<Backed>. Methods that produce an empty stream create TokenStream<Empty>. This removes runtime Some/None checks and makes misuse (e.g., expand_expr on Empty) a compile error.
